$comment: Registry of browsers/OSes ported to the web via v86 (WASM CPU + canvas + Wisp networking). Image URLs are relative to the CDN host (default https://i.copy.sh/, overridable with ?cdn=). The `config` object mirrors v86 constructor options; the runner prepends the CDN host to every disk/state `url`. An image may also carry a `mirror` path (resilience: a same-origin copy under dist/mirror/); the runner prefers it unless a `?cdn=` is forced.
$relays_comment: Wisp relays offered in the runner's relay picker (first = default). Each was verified to speak Wisp (sends the v1 CONTINUE handshake frame on connect). The runner also offers a Custom… option. v86 maps wisps:// → wss://.

browsers.name: KolibriOS
browsers.blurb: Tiny assembly-written OS that boots from a single 1.44 MB floppy in seconds. Ships a real graphical web browser (WebView) with TCP/IP networking.
browsers.hint: Open WEBVIEW (or NETSURF) from the desktop to start browsing.

browsers.name: Windows 2000
browsers.blurb: Microsoft Windows 2000 Professional resumed from a saved state, with the real Internet Explorer 5 / Trident engine. Also bundles K-Meleon (Gecko), Lynx and Retrozilla. Networked through Wisp.
browsers.hint: First double-click 'networking.bat' on the desktop, then open Internet Explorer (or K-Meleon / Lynx / Retrozilla).

browsers.name: Windows 95
browsers.blurb: The OS that put a Start button on the world. Windows 95 resumed from a saved state, running Internet Explorer (Trident). A genuine 1990s browsing experience entirely in your tab.
browsers.hint: Open Internet Explorer from the desktop. If a page won't load, enable networking first (a 'networking.bat' may be on the desktop).

browsers.name: Windows 98
browsers.blurb: Windows 98 resumed from a saved state with Internet Explorer. A widely-used browser of its era running entirely in your tab.
browsers.hint: First double-click 'networking.bat' on the desktop, then open Internet Explorer.

browsers.name: Windows ME
browsers.blurb: Windows Millennium Edition resumed from a saved state, with Internet Explorer.
browsers.hint: Enable networking (look for 'networking.bat' on the desktop), then open Internet Explorer.

browsers.name: Haiku
browsers.blurb: Haiku is a modern reimagining of BeOS. Its native browser, WebPositive, is built on WebKit, so it renders the modern web. Also includes the Links browser. Resumed from a saved state.
browsers.hint: In the Terminal, run 'networking.sh' to enable networking, then open WebPositive.

browsers.name: ReactOS
browsers.blurb: ReactOS is an open-source OS binary-compatible with Windows. Boots from a saved state to a familiar desktop. Networked through Wisp.
browsers.hint: Open a web browser from the Start menu; enable networking if prompted.

browsers.name: SerenityOS
browsers.blurb: SerenityOS ships Ladybird, a from-scratch independent browser engine (LibWeb/LibJS). Resumed from a saved state.
browsers.hint: Open Ladybird (or Browser) from the taskbar.

browsers.name: 9front
browsers.blurb: 9front is an actively-developed fork of Plan 9 from Bell Labs, the research OS that grew out of Unix. It ships the native Mothra web browser (and a port of NetSurf), resumed here from a saved state. A genuinely unusual engine running in your tab.
browsers.hint: Right-click the desktop to open the menu; launch the 'mothra' web browser (or NetSurf). Networking is configured at boot.

browsers.name: Redox OS
browsers.blurb: Redox is a modern, Unix-like operating system written from scratch in Rust around a microkernel, with its own Orbital GUI desktop. Its demo image ships the NetSurf web browser. Resumed here from a saved state and auto-logged-in to the Orbital desktop.
browsers.hint: You'll be auto-logged-in. From the Orbital desktop, open NetSurf (Browser) from the bottom taskbar / launcher. (Note: this fast state-restore image has no working NIC driver, so live browsing may not connect.)

browsers.name: SliTaz GNU/Linux
browsers.blurb: SliTaz is a tiny (under 60 MB) but complete GNU/Linux distribution that boots a full graphical desktop from a live CD. It ships Midori, a WebKitGTK browser (plus SliTaz's own WebKit-based TazWeb). DHCP networking over Wisp comes up automatically.
browsers.hint: Wait for the desktop, then right-click it (or use the panel) and open Midori under Favorite Applications / Internet. Networking is automatic.

browsers.name: Android 4.4 (KitKat)
browsers.blurb: Android-x86 4.4 'KitKat' running the stock AOSP Browser, a WebKit-based engine. Android is one of the most widely used platforms in the world; here it runs entirely in your tab. Networked through Wisp.
browsers.hint: Patience: it streams ~250 MB and the full Android boot takes ~4-5 minutes in software emulation. When the home screen appears, dismiss the welcome card and open the Browser app.

browsers.name: Damn Small Linux
browsers.blurb: A 50 MB Linux live CD that bundles the Dillo and Firefox browsers. Boots into X11 from CD-ROM.
browsers.hint: It boots automatically; once the X11 desktop appears, launch Dillo or Firefox from the desktop / menu.
